title: Lesson 193
transcript: All right, so in this module, we're going to apply the concept of architectures into an entire enterprise. And if we remember what the word architecture itself means, it means a whole bunch of components that work together to allow us to use the architecture for purposes for which it was intended. And so if we take an entire architecture and break it down into components, when we want to secure the entire architecture, we're going to do the same thing. So we're going to take different components and break it down and protect each one. That's really what an architecture is. It's a whole bunch of components. Now if we add the word security to it, security means that each of the components that make up the entire architecture are protected as much as we need, usually based on value. Now if we apply this to an entire company, That's where the word enterprise comes in. An enterprise is the entire company itself, and it's also made up of different components. So when you look at entire company, an enterprise, It's going to be made up of people, processes, technologies and network and data, for example. And so if you want to protect an entire company, The way that we do it is we break it down into the major components, protect each one based on value of each, and that's what we call a enterprise security architecture. So the same concepts that we talked about before in how we protect any architecture, whether it's really small or whether it's a large enterprise, a company that may be global, for example. We protect it the same way by breaking it down into components and that's what we refer to as an enterprise security architecture. Now protecting companies is nothing new. We've been doing that for decades and so the industries have come up with frameworks to facilitate all of this and help companies achieve enterprise security architecture. And as far as how many there exists, there are really three significant ones that the industry has come up with. And what you see on the screen here are the three major enterprise security architectures that we've come up with. And they're listed sort of in a chronological order. And so if you look at the history of enterprise security architectures, the very first one that was developed is called Zachman. And then that was followed by something called Sabsa. And the latest one, which seems to be the one that companies prefer to use because of what companies look like today, is actually called TOGAF. But when you look at the way that these are structured, They really look very similar because they do exactly the same thing that we've been talking about in how you protect architectures. So if you remember the concept of breaking things down into specific components and then protecting each of the components, it's exactly what these enterprise security architectures do. So if you look at the very first one here which was developed in the early 70s, it's a way to take the entire company, break it down into components that are represented here by each of the levels or layers that you see and so the idea is you look at each of the components and you ask the same questions for example in what security needs to be able to do in order to help achieve security for each of the different layers or components so the idea is you ask the same questions and of all of the different components for example but if you look at different layers of what any company looks like they're going to give you different answers to those questions. So for example, if you ask, you know, upper management, what do they want security to be able to do for them? they're going to give you different answers than if you ask that same question to, for example, the operations people or users in specific departments, for example. And so this is a way to capture in a two-dimensional perspective the answers to what security needs to be able to address at different levels of the organization so that we can capture everything in this two-dimensional table format and then analyze that information to be able to address the security requirements. The problem with Zachman is that It was developed for companies in the way that companies looked back in the 70s. And back then, companies looked much different than they do today. And so the industry had to take this and adapt it to what companies look like today. And that's what really SABSA provides for us. It's a different view of the components that really make up what companies look like today. so that again we can ask questions related to security at different levels of the organization to capture relevant information that can then be analyzed through the SABSA process to be able to design and implement security requirements at different levels of the organization. and the latest one is actually called TOGAF which obviously stands for the Open Group Architecture Framework and even though it looks a little bit different than the previous two that we just showed you It's really the same concept where you see the word requirements in the middle here, but all of the outside pieces are each of the components that really represent value to the organization itself. And so again, it's a way for us to understand the security requirements based on value for each of the components that makes up the entire enterprise or company and understand those from a goals and objectives perspective so that we can design ultimately security that's built into every major component of an enterprise and address security for the same. In other words, whether you're using a small architecture like a laptop, or a really large organization that is global, the way that we approach security is exactly the same way. And that is to break it down into individual components that represent value to the organization and not only understand the security requirements but be able to design and implement those in the best way possible which means obviously the most efficient and cost-effective way possible to be able to implement security. These are three significant enterprise security architecture models that allow companies to achieve enterprise security architecture.
